result:
The spiral was a catechism etched in iron and stone. Silas knew its verses by heart. One hundred and thirty-eight steps, each one a slight, concave dip worn smooth by the boots of his father, and his father’s father. The air was thick with the ghosts of their labour—the faint scent of oil, salt, and cold, damp stone. His own breath, a small, white cloud in the narrow shaft, joined theirs.

He was the last. The last keeper of the St. Jude’s Reach Lighthouse.

At the top, the lantern room was a glass chapel suspended between a turbulent sea and an indifferent sky. He ran a calloused hand over the brass housing of the great lens, a Fresnel marvel of prismatic glass that looked like a giant, crystalline beehive. To him, it was not an object. It was a heart. When he flicked the switch, it would begin its slow, rhythmic pulse, a heartbeat of light throwing its steady promise across the water. Three seconds on, five seconds off. A language everyone in the darkness understood.

The mainland called him a relic. An anachronism. They had their GPS, their satellite navigation, their digital charts that painted the seafloor in perfect, sterile detail. A man in a tower with a big lightbulb was a fairy tale, a romantic notion that cost the Authority too much in pension and supply runs. The letters came every six months now, phrased in polite, bureaucratic jargon that all meant the same thing: *When are you going to give up and let us turn this into a museum?*

Silas would burn them in the small potbelly stove that fought a losing battle against the Atlantic chill. He wasn’t guarding a light; he was guarding a promise. GPS could tell you where you were, but it couldn’t look you in the eye. It couldn’t offer a pinprick of warm, tangible hope in the face of a howling gale. It was a ghost in the machine. His light was a hand on the shoulder.

He remembered a storm from his childhood, hiding under the great oak chart table while his father stood watch. The lighthouse had groaned like a great beast in pain. Waves, the size of houses, had detonated against the granite base, sending shudders all the way up to the lantern room. The wind didn’t howl; it screamed, a high, thin sound of pure malice. Through it all, his father had been a statue, his silhouette framed against the sweeping beam, a dark, unmovable constant against the chaos. He didn’t just operate the light; he willed it to stay lit. He was in communion with it.

That was what the men in their comfortable mainland offices would never understand. This wasn't a job. It was a vocation, a symbiotic relationship. The light needed a keeper, and the keeper, in his profound and solitary existence, needed the light.

He stepped out onto the narrow gallery that ringed the lantern. The wind whipped his grey beard across his face and stole the warmth from his woollen coat. Below, the sea was a great, rumpled blanket of bruised purple, flecked with white. It was a living thing, ancient and moody, and he had spent his life learning its tempers. He knew the sound of a coming squall by the change in pitch as the wind passed through the iron railings. He could smell snow on the air hours before the first flake fell. This rock, this tower, this circle of churning water was his entire world, and it was a world of infinite complexity.

Sometimes, on clear nights, he saw the faint, glittering smear of the town on the horizon. He imagined people in their warm houses, watching television, arguing about politics, falling in love, forgetting he was out here. They were a different species, living lives of tangled, noisy connection. His connections were simpler, more profound. The cry of a gannet. The slick, dark head of a seal surfacing in the cove. The steady, reassuring sweep of the beam from the lighthouse twenty miles down the coast, a silent conversation between brothers.

He had never married. A woman had come to the island once, a journalist doing a story on "The Last Lightkeepers." She was bright and quick, full of questions he didn't have the words to answer. How could he explain the feeling of responsibility when the fog rolled in so thick it felt like you could scoop it with your hands? How could he describe the sacred quiet after a storm, when the world was scrubbed clean and new? She saw loneliness. He saw solitude. She saw an empty life. He saw a full one. She left after three days, and he never saw her again.

Tonight, the air was sharp and clear. The first stars were beginning to prick the darkening velvet of the sky. It was time. He went back inside and stood before the switchboard. His hand hovered over the large, black lever. He thought of the ships out there, the tankers and trawlers and tiny fishing boats. They were all depending on this. On him. A man his own government had deemed obsolete.

He threw the switch.

There was a low hum, and then the world outside his glass chapel was flooded with a clean, powerful luminescence. The great lens began to turn, silent and smooth on its mercury bath. The beam slid across his face, momentarily blinding him, before continuing its journey out over the water. Three seconds on. He counted in his head. *One… two… three…* The light cut out, plunging the world back into twilight. *One… two… three… four… five…* And it returned, a faithful, brilliant flare.

A promise made. A promise kept.

The letters from the Authority could keep coming. The world could keep spinning on its digital axis. But out here, in the vast, breathing dark, some things still needed a human heart to keep them turning. He would hold the dark at bay. One more night. And then one more.
